blocks.text: [[explorer.foundation.thesis|Implementation is cheap; expert attention is the bottleneck.]] When code is written faster than it is read, one failure dominates: **code that passes every check and is still wrong.** The tools that detect this are decades old — what was never affordable is the labor of acting on what they find. Agents changed that.

PDD is a method for changing code you can't trust — legacy, or AI-written — without breaking it. Every practice in it is held to one standard: run, trigger, or leave a record — [[explorer.pillar.trustworthy-green|gates that fail the build in block mode]], skills that fire mid-task, [[term.ledger|decisions in a replayable ledger]] — so the machine's work is validated, not trusted, and human judgment is spent at a few recorded decision points. Five moves: [[explorer.verb.map|MAP]] recovers what the code does, [[explorer.verb.pin|PIN]] locks it in tests, [[explorer.verb.carve|CARVE]] cuts work to attention size, [[explorer.verb.ship|SHIP]] changes code under gates, [[explorer.verb.prove|PROVE]] confirms by mutation that the tests would catch a regression.
Code can pass every check and still be wrong. Two causes:

items: [[explorer.failure.adequacy-gap|Tests that pass but don't constrain the behavior]] — they would pass even if the code were wrong. Coverage can't detect this: a line that ran is not a line a test would fail for.
[[explorer.failure.visibility-gap|Errors handled silently]] — a broken system that still looks healthy.

blocks.text: Review can catch both, at the cost of expert attention — the one resource that doesn't scale with generated code. PDD makes the checks mechanical: [[explorer.pillar.trustworthy-green|a verdict that fails the build]] — the static checks cheap to re-run, the mutation pass explicitly budgeted — so attention goes only where judgment is needed.

Each move closes one way the failure survives: MAP because the names mislead; PIN because there is no spec until you write one; CARVE because oversized work gets skimmed, not reviewed; SHIP because a convention that is not enforced in CI erodes; PROVE because a green suite is a claim that has to be audited.

sections.title: MAP — recover what the system does

blocks.text: **Why.** In untrusted code the names lie — `validate` functions that don't, `cleanup` that leaks. A plan built on the names inherits their errors, so the first move is [[skill-read-the-system|read the implementation, not the signature]].

**What.** For the operator: the [[term.system-truth-atlas|System Truth Atlas]] — verified code map, [[term.footgun|footgun register]], recovered-risk register, [[term.silent-failure|silent-failure census]]. For the machine: a problem list ([[artifact.merged-findings|findings]]) that the next moves consume risk-first.
**How.** [[doc-reference-pdd-map|`pdd map`]] runs three layers in cost order ([[code.spine|the spine]]), like a CI pipeline — cheap stages first:

items: *Mechanical* — the enabled static gates (nine in the registry), then targeted mutation at the boundaries the tests claim to cover.
*Deterministic* — subsystems ranked by [[code.scope-ranking|finding density and git churn]], so the expensive read starts where the risk concentrates.

*Agentic* — what static analysis can't detect (a `validate` that returns early, a swallowed error that matters), an agent finds by reading, highest-ranked subsystem first. Its findings are data, and the CLI validates them at [[concept.write-boundary|the write boundary]] — input validation, applied to the agent.

blocks.text: **Proof.** A finding that cites a nonexistent source file is rejected before write. The mechanical layer adds no randomness of its own; re-runs are as reproducible as the project's test suite. The downstream carve is byte-identical on unchanged input, so a re-map's diff shows what changed (plus a run timestamp).
MAP hands PIN a problem list and a model of the system accurate enough to lock behavior against.

sections.subtitle: Standard practice: design recovery, plus a static-analysis and mutation sweep.

sections.title: PIN — lock current behavior

blocks.text: **Why.** There is no spec. The code's current behavior — bugs included — is the only truth, and until it is captured, [[explorer.failure.behavior-drift|drift is undetectable]]: a change can break behavior nobody wrote down while the suite stays green.

**What.** Two locks. [[term.characterization-test|Characterization tests]] pin the behaviors a change could disturb, held to the same adequacy bar as any test ([[term.boundary-test|boundary case]], [[term.no-op-path|no-op path]], pinned values) — a happy-path-only pin constrains nothing. A [[term.reproducibility-baseline|reproducibility baseline]] pins the whole output: replay-and-diff where the system is deterministic, golden master where it isn't yet. A bug found while pinning is captured and flagged (expected-fail), not encoded as correct.

**How.** [[skill-characterize-before-change|Skills]] trigger during the change itself. Where determinism is missing, the clock, RNG, and I/O are injected as [[term.seam|seams]] — dependency injection at the test boundary — and making the system reproducible counts as a deliverable, not test overhead.

**Proof.** A pin is only real if it fails when behavior changes, and that is checked rather than assumed: SHIP's pin-values gate rejects assertions on a type or truthiness where a value was the point, and PROVE mutates the code under the pin to confirm the test notices. PIN has no CLI verb; it is enforced by what runs downstream.
PIN hands CARVE a locked baseline: change is detectable, so it can be planned.

sections.subtitle: Standard practice: characterization tests plus a golden master, held to an adequacy bar.

sections.title: CARVE — cut work to attention size

blocks.text: **Why.** Work sized past attention doesn't get reviewed, it gets skimmed. A plan built on an unproven architectural assumption fails late, where rework is most expensive.

**What.** The remediation roadmap ([[artifact.units-json-carve-baseline|units.json]]): the problem list carved into [[term.unit|units]] — grouped by subsystem, [[term.attention-sized|banded to attention size]] (an oversized unit is flagged for a finer carve), risk-ranked, each rank traceable to the findings that produced it. For plans carrying architectural risk, a [[skill-derisk-gate|de-risk gate]]: a thin end-to-end spike proves the riskiest assumption before anything is built on it, ending in a GREEN / RED / OVERRIDE verdict.

**How.** The carve is [[code.carve|a pure function]] of the findings and the grouping: same input, byte-identical units — reproducible-build discipline applied to a planning artifact. Cost is the third sizing axis after unit- and PR-attention: an opt-in [[term.gate-budget|gate budget]] bounds each unit's estimated mutation cost in machine-minutes, calibrated from [[artifact.calibration-ledger|real runs]]; over-budget groups split until they fit or are atomic, and un-splittable ones are flagged (warn-first today).

**Proof.** Each rank persists its [[term.riskscore|computed basis]] on the unit, so the ordering is traceable to inputs. Estimates are recorded against actuals, so the cost model is audited by its own history.
CARVE hands the operator the roadmap. The triage itself is deliberately manual.

sections.subtitle: Standard practice: work decomposition plus a de-risking spike — sized to reviewer attention, not story points.

blocks.text: The machine has mapped, locked, and carved. It never approves or denies a unit — that decision is concentrated at one gate instead of distributed across every diff.

At [[term.curate-gate|the curate gate]] the operator triages each unit: approve, deny (rationale required), edit, [[term.disposition|split, move, or consume into a refactor]]. The roadmap is event-sourced: every decision appends to [[artifact.roadmap-ledger-audit|a ledger]], and the effective state is derived by replaying it over the untouched carve baseline — so a re-map never overwrites a decision, and any state reconstructs from history. Undo appends a reverting entry: git-revert semantics, never a rewrite.
[[term.sign-off|Sign-off]] closes the gate. It requires every unit approved or denied — pending blocks it — and freezes the roadmap: further edits are refused until an explicit reopen.
The run/trigger/record standard, applied to the human: judgment is a small set of recorded decisions that downstream stages are gated on.

sections.title: SHIP — change code under gates

blocks.text: **Why.** A convention enforced only in review comments does not survive throughput. The line holds where crossing it fails the build.

**What.** [[doc-reference-pdd-check-and-the-gates|Nine gates]], each catching one shape of green-but-wrong: a threshold no test references, a conditional cleanup with no test for the no-trigger path, an acquire with [[term.cover-the-mirror|no release]], a swallowed error, an assertion on a type where a value was the point. Modes (block / warn / off) live in committed config ([[artifact.constitution|the constitution]]) — the same trust model as branch protection: versioned, visible, enforced. The human half: each change ships as one operator-reviewable milestone.

**How.** [[code.gates-registry|`pdd check`]] runs in CI as a merge gate (a pre-commit hook running `check --staged` ships in `.pdd/hooks/` for one-line install); language-agnostic core gates plus [[term.language-pack|language packs]] behind a JSON contract (TypeScript and Python today). Most gates ship as warn and are promoted to block once their precision is proven on the codebase — the gate earns enforcement.
**Proof.** The verdict is an exit code, deterministic on re-run. The gates are proxies, and the proxy is audited: that is the next move.

sections.subtitle: Standard practice: CI quality gates — on test adequacy, not coverage.

sections.title: PROVE — audit the green

blocks.text: **Why.** A green suite is a claim, not a fact. SHIP's gates report a boundary "covered"; only injecting the bug shows whether any test would fail.

**What.** Two commands with different jobs. [[code.prove|`pdd prove`]] is a targeted oracle: at each boundary the proxy gate marked covered, it flips the comparison operator and runs that boundary's tests — a survivor is a [[term.false-covered-boundary|false-covered boundary]] and fails the build. [[term.mutation-grading|`pdd grade`]] is a broad diagnostic: [[code.mutators|nine bug-shaped operators]] over a scope, producing a score and [[artifact.unit-mutation-survivors|a survivor list]] in which each entry is either a weak test to strengthen or dead code to delete.

**How.** No verdict is issued over a failing baseline. `grade` results are cached by content hash (`prove` re-runs each time); parallel grading runs in isolated git worktrees, with a crash-safe in-place fallback when a sandbox can't produce a verdict; a sampled run is marked sampled and excluded from cost calibration.

**Proof.** A killed mutant is direct evidence a pin holds. A survivor is a specific, reproducible counterexample — actionable per item, unlike an aggregate score. The verdict is exit 0 or 1; a run that can't produce one (red baseline, no test command) exits 2 instead of guessing.

sections.subtitle: Standard practice: mutation testing, tied to the no-drift baseline from PIN.

blocks.text: Findings are not the deliverable; merged fixes are. [[doc-reference-pdd-remediate|`pdd remediate`]] runs the approved roadmap and refuses to start without [[term.sign-off|sign-off]].

Per unit, in risk order: an isolated worktree; a fix authored by a provider behind an interface (deterministic stand-ins today — the agent provider is the open piece); then the fixer's output is validated like everything else — the diff is [[code.remediate-loop|scope-checked]] against the unit, the check gates re-run against the post-fix code (prove/grade findings are not yet re-swept — those units route to the operator), the pinned suite guards regressions when the project has one, and only a certified-green fix is committed. The review model is a dependabot branch: one commit per unit with provenance trailers, the tool commits, the operator merges. Any attempt that produces an in-scope diff persists its patch before gating, so rejected fixes keep their review record.
`committed` means awaiting merge, not resolved. Whether a fix landed is determined by re-running MAP — the finding clears and the re-carve drops the unit — not by trusting a status field.
The loop: recover, lock, carve, judge, change, prove, re-map. Caches (atlas, mutation, check) and the decision ledger carry state across passes, so a re-pass pays only for what changed.
